title: RDI backlog, memory pressure, and backpressure
summary: Explains how stream backlog consumes memory and how RDI backpressure slows intake so the pipeline can recover.

question_patterns: Why does the Debezium log say out of memory during snapshot?

nodes.label: RDI stream append rate
nodes.description: The rate at which captured events are written to Redis streams in the RDI database.

nodes.label: Processor throughput
nodes.description: The rate at which records complete read, transform, write, and acknowledgement steps.

nodes.label: RDI stream backlog
nodes.description: Captured events waiting in RDI streams to be processed, written to the target, and acknowledged.

nodes.label: Stream event latency
nodes.description: The age of the oldest unprocessed event in each RDI data stream.

nodes.label: RDI database memory pressure
nodes.description: Memory pressure from staged change events and RDI state in the Redis Enterprise cluster.

nodes.label: Backpressure level
nodes.description: The degree to which RDI slows or stops incoming data to protect the streams and target side of the pipeline.

nodes.label: Collector intake rate
nodes.description: The rate at which the collector accepts source records for the pipeline.

nodes.label: Changes held at source
nodes.description: Change data that remains at the source while RDI is throttled and will be captured when intake resumes.

nodes.label: Lag behind source
nodes.description: How far the pipeline is behind the source database in time.

edges.condition: Append rate exceeds completed processing rate.
edges.summary: Events appended to RDI streams increase backlog when they arrive faster than the stream processor completes them.

edges.condition: Completed throughput exceeds stream append rate.
edges.summary: When processing completes faster than events arrive, the RDI stream backlog falls.
A larger backlog tends to increase the age of the oldest unprocessed stream event.
A growing stream backlog consumes more memory in the RDI database.

edges.condition: RDI detects that stream growth could exhaust available memory.
edges.summary: RDI increases backpressure when backlog and memory pressure threaten the pipeline.
Backpressure slows or stops incoming data from the collector.
When intake is throttled, change data remains at the source until RDI has capacity to resume.
Held source changes increase the time lag between the source and the RDI pipeline.

feedback_loops.label: Memory protection through backpressure
feedback_loops.summary: When stream backlog raises memory pressure, RDI applies backpressure. Backpressure reduces collector intake, which lowers future stream appends and gives the processor time to clear backlog.

causal_chains.question: How does backpressure act on the flow of data?
causal_chains.summary: Backpressure is a protective control. It reduces collector intake when backlog threatens memory, leaving change data at the source and increasing lag until the processor catches up.

diagnostic_hints.interpretation: RDI is protecting the pipeline by slowing or stopping intake. This indicates delayed capture, not necessarily lost changes.

answering_guidance: Explain backpressure as protective throttling, not data loss by default.
Mention that change data is held at the source until RDI clears the backlog and has enough free memory to resume streaming.
Treat Debezium out-of-memory messages during initial snapshot as possible evidence that backpressure is active, not automatically as a fatal error.
